1 AI Governance   
by Vidhi Chugh \- [https://www.linkedin.com/learning/introduction-to-ai-governance/developing-the-lens-of-ai-governance?u=67698794](https://www.linkedin.com/learning/introduction-to-ai-governance/developing-the-lens-of-ai-governance?u=67698794) 

| Governance Landscapes |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Governance | A comprehensive approach to regulate and govern advanced AI systems Promotes responsible and ethical practices throughout the AI lifecycle  Make decisions to \- develop, deploy and monitor AI systems to ensure they perform as intended  AI Governance is the overarching term  ![][image1] |
| AI Benefits | AI benefits Has high potential in interpreting languages like never before  Is taking centrestage discussion in boardrooms Is disrupting businesses	 |
| AI risks | Risks existed even before Gen AI era  Risks existing from pre-generative AI inheriting or at times amplifying bias from the training data violating privacy by exposing sensitive user information without consent,  lack of transparency in AI's decision making Loss of jobs that need basic cognitive skills Additional risks from GenAI that can create new data \- unique risks and challenges GenAI creating new content leads to Deepfakes make it difficult to identify what is real  Cyberattacks Misinformation / disinformation Intellectual property infringement \- GenAI systems may create content that could closely resemble existing copyrighted material, leading to concerns around intellectual property rights. *(but if we have all been fractional contributors to GenAI models training data, then who really owns the Intellectual Property rights to AI?)* |
| What do most people think of Governance ? | Think that it is restrictive Hinders business growth  In reality, Governance  allows us to foster innovation by harnessing the upside of AI while effectively managing its potential risk |
| Various terms | regulations, laws, guidelines, policies, norms, and ethical practices How are all these related? \- there’s a whole spectrum ![][image2]![][image3] ![][image4]  |
| AI Act by European Union | 1st comprehensive approach to regulating AI  Started as a general approach Policy, blueprint to set the underlying principles to build trustworthy AI → policies do not have a force of law → and gains legal enforcement status after going through rigorous legislative journey  |
| Effective Governance requires certain details like | Details that are needed are:  Use cases to be defined Disclosure of training data Defining Risk Defining Risk Mitigation measures Identifying risks from training data or bad actors  Understanding consequences and compliance needed *(penalties of non-compliance of the AI Act going up to 40 million euros or 7% global turnover)* Knowing who needs to apply  Regulations bring clarity on such aspects and enable a policy to become a Law   |
| Evaluating AI Systems \- avenues to check for, to ensure setting up for governance | Are there common standards to check for bias identification and evaluation In what ways could the systems be audited for compliance?  What would an ideal governance model look like How and what documentation will be established for the model details?  What key areas to look for while building models today?  How to embed transparency and accountability by design?  Find legal counsel or subject matter experts to ensure full compliance  Ethics by design \- Ethical design thinking  |
| Governance Example  | Understand the context of the example The measures and organization can take  Assess impact to users Assess impact to business  |
| Case 1 \- AI-generated interactions  | **Context** \- AI has remarkable language generation capability **Governance Measures**  Notify AI interaction or AI content  Help exercise caution and make informed decisions  **Impact to user**, being aware of AI being involved in that interaction can  Allow users to consider AI limitations, lack of human insight, intuition or context  This impact becomes more acute in critical domains like Finance and healthcare  **Impact to business** \- Governance helps to build customer trust and brand loyalty Gives business the competitive edge  |
| Case 2 \- Chatbots | **Context \-** relevant natural conversational capability, but lack of Governance could harm business **Governance Measures** \-  Build mechanisms to audit, monitor and filter any inappropriate response is critical Solicit user feedback and fine-tune  **Impact to users** Provide users with a safe channel to ask questions  Provide effective query resolution and positive customer experience  **Impact to Business** Shows org’s focus on Compliance and ethical measures Builds credibility  |
| Hence, | AI Governance does not hinder progress, rather promotes innovations ![][image5] |
| Need for increasing Governance | As AI has expanded, the associated risks have expanded too Needs stricter measures that form the basis of Governance  |
| Some bad results of AI | A movie recommender did not recommend good movies  Product search did not return relevant results Biased algorithms targeted specific communities to deny them credit loans or employment, granting of bail or access to healthcare  AI algorithm adjusted scores based on the school’s status  Bias based on economic status  False alerts for AI being used for assignment   |
| What could have been done | Identify Fairness to be one of the model evaluation criteria Timely audits and rigorous testing is important  Disclosure of its usage should have been done  There must be safeguards in place to appeal the ruling with clear process on how to do this \- for e.g. if a student was accused of using AI in an assignment, there should have been a process for the student to plea to over-rule the ruling   There should have been other alternate ways of working  Innocents should not be negatively impacted  |
| Dimensions of Governance | Stakeholders in the Governance process  End users \- directly affected by AI systems Technical teams \- building AI apps Business stakeholders \- impacted by AI implementations and outcomes Government bodies \- creating and enforcing regulations  Global nature of AI  As LLMs train on almost from entire world’s data, Governance needs international collaboration and standardization  Globally interoperable rules that transcends boundaries  The global nature reduce the fear that AI innovation would be driven to other nations with fewer regulations  Analyse for sector-specific risks  High-impact sectors call for stringent governance  Segregate risk source \- is it the algorithm or the underlying data?  Unintended factors  Like unexpected interactions Previous unseen patterns emerging Deliberate factors  Someone or something trying to sabotage applications Exploiting vulnerabilities  Extracting sensitive information, violating privacy  Organisations struggle with the challenge of \- How does the regulation segregate between an accidental or unintended outcome from AI applications versus its deliberate misuse? |
| Risk categories  | ![][image6] Why to categorise risks?  Address high priority risks first Minimise impact to users, stakeholders and customers Bring in necessary accountability  Avoid hefty penalties in case of violations  |
| Embedding ethics by design | Ethics must be conducted upfront, at ideation stage,  and not as an afterthought The abstract nature of ethics creates a challenge How to put business ethical considerations into ML code \- is a challenge for developers  Ethics needs an organisation wide change \- to define the right roles and principles Getting answers upfront to What it means to be ‘Fair’ Info about the internals of an algorithm Facilitate trust Who should audit What should be the training set Does the data represent fair results Is the data a good representation of the problem to be solved  What is the criteria to test model performance, bias, behave as intended   |
| Challenges of governing AI | Global AI governance is a global problem, needing global consensus, dialogues are ongoing Risks are rapidly emerging, so there is a need to act quicker Promote equitable and trustworthy AI  Diversity \- regional perspectives and opinions  Avoidance of Governance Silos is needed Benefits and harms are infused \- e.g. facial recognition is used for authentication like at airport checkins, but, there are privacy concerns for individuals.  If this data is used to train algorithms then it could be real threat for individuals   |
| Steps to set up Governance | Developers, teams, Organisation, Law \- all the multiple layers involved |
| Set up an Ethics board | **Absence** of Ethics board will lead to focus from human-centric to profit-centric Ethics board values are driven by the organization’s values and vision Any AI powered product must be run through an ethics board at the idea or proposal phase  Keep the Ethics board involved in key stages of the product  Compliance, Legal, Engineering, Product, data science \- from diverse groups should be part of the Ethics board  Emerging Ethics board profiles:  ![][image7]  |
| When do Ethics boards become ineffective? | When they are perceived as campaign agenda for marketing Need organizational culture change beyond superficial engagements Board must be able to act and approve independently \- and not be pressured by people running for business profitability etc.  Robust policies should be in place to insulate  |
| Identify and mitigate AI risks | Handling critical risks, bias and privacy  Bias ML models must be trained on diverse training data for all categories  When a diverse group assesses the solutions they can immediately identify who/which categories are missing from the data and may face adverse outcomes  It is crucial to identify if there is a gap  Privacy  user preferences, habits, choices, demographics are privacy data Data breaches can cause violations and penalties  Data breach in bad hands \- needs high severity handling  Ask for clear user consent, not make it ambiguous  Measures in place to prevent data theft and breaches   |
| Drive organisational governance | Governance at the development level  Self-regulation by developers Discuss with teams to leverage diverse technical expertise  Ethics board need to be involved at all crucial milestones for assessment and feedback Business stakeholders decide the way forward  Independent audits need to be involved to help, assess and validate Instill the right governance principles  Enable teams to build systems and processes, compliance workflows, documentation of algorithms, details for audit compliance, define and quantify risk  Threshold of residual risk that is acceptable Team should know what to do incase of any issues  Definition and ability to quantify risk  |
| Road ahead | Global governance is being discussed at a global scale  EU AI Act is expected to be in full force in 2026  US standard \- AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) ![][image8]![][image9] |
| Change Management | GenAI is a transformative force  Revised ways of working are needed with streamlined processes and policies to ramp up their digital transformation journeys  A more reasoned approach is needed for AI, rather considering it as a magic quick-fix Knowing capabilities & possibilities along with limitations & challenges Organisation must be open about AI plans \- tough time ahead for manual repetitive job streams  Fear of displacement from workforce is real ![][image10] ![][image11] Looks like change management is also a key area?\!  |
| Advancing AI governance in your organisation | ![][image12]![][image13] |
